title: Penn State baseball commit Alex Weingartner selected by Chicago White Sox in 2026 MLB Draft
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A potential future Nittany Lion has gone off the board.

Penn State commit Alex Weingartner was chosen in the sixth round by the Chicago White Sox in the 2026 MLB Draft.

Weingartner was listed as a position player when selected, but also has pitching potential that makes him a highly sought-after recruit and prospect.

The New Jersey native reaches the upper 90s on his fastball while also holding plus speed on the base paths.

Weingartner joins his brother, and former Penn State shortstop, Ryan Weingartner, as MLB draft picks. Alex Weingartner's selection marks the second player with Penn State connections joining designated hitter Michael Anderson as draftees.

It's unknown whether Alex Weingartner will honor his commitment to Penn State or forgo his college career and join the White Sox organization. If he plays for the Nittany Lions, he could be reselected in the MLB draft in 2029.

title: Penn State baseball's Michael Anderson selected by Texas Rangers in 2026 MLB Draft
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Gambino has sent another player to the big leagues.

Penn State pitcher and designated hitter Michael Anderson was selected by the Texas Rangers in the fifth round of the 2026 MLB Draft.

While Anderson only played at Penn State for one season, he led the Nittany Lions at the plate all year. Anderson previously played for Rhode Island and Arkansas before throwing on blue and white threads — totaling 103 career games and 99 starts as an infielder at those schools.

Throughout his season at Penn State, Anderson totaled 53 RBI’s, 68 hits, and 11 doubles in 50 total games. He also made two appearances on the mound before injuring his arm, throwing 1.2 innings and striking out a batter.

Anderson’s offensive efforts this season earned him first-team All-Big Ten honors, ranking him second for home runs in the Big Ten Conference, with 20 total.

The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native will join former Penn State center fielder Paxton Kling in the Rangers' system.

Anderson continuously dominates as a right-handed hitter, excelling at consistent hit production. He was selected as a two-way player, giving him the opportunity to hit and pitch in the minor leagues.

A former Penn State player is joining his fourth team in his collegiate career.

Forward Saša Ciani has transferred to Sacramento State, the team announced Friday.

Ciani spent last season with the Nittany Lions, playing in 28 games. He began the year with four-straight starts but became a rotational player in the frontcourt after freshman Ivan Jurić took over the starting center duties.

title: Off the record | Mind the gap
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear reader,

Every relationship, platonic, romantic or otherwise, is a direct reflection of the standards we hold for ourselves and how we are treated. Even though it’s apparent in friendships, it needs to be at the forefront of our minds when picking a partner. There’s absolutely no reason for you to settle for someone who doesn’t have a basic level of respect for you.

There’s a plethora of things that I could say inspired this, but a couple of months ago, I was walking in downtown State College, and I watched a girl fall off the curb in her high heels. The guy (I assume her date) stood there. He then looked down and kept walking. Yes, he just kept walking.

It’s actually quite repulsive, and I can only hope she’s no longer with him. But let this serve as a learning experience for us all. Your standards while dating need to be astronomically high. Not, “Oh, he held the door open for me one time,” but expecting the very best from the person you spend your time with.

Maybe this is hitting a little too close to home, and if it is, I need you to go ahead and do the thing we’re both thinking.

This doesn’t make you a snob, or picky. You’re allowed to want the best of the best. I know people who let their partners get away with the bare minimum, who let their partners get praised for things I would do for my friends with zero hesitation.

No, a gap isn’t taking a break from the person themselves, but rather noticing the gap between a quality that you possess and one they don’t. Most of my favorite people are in relationships where there’s a humor gap. She’s the funniest person I’ve ever met, and he’s a bore.

I wish this were a concept I, myself, magically created. Instead, I owe credit to the girls on TikTok who recognize there are no gaps in their relationships. They’ll post something with their partner that highlights the lack of a “humor gap,” “intelligence gap,” or “political gap.” This is the perfect phrasing, because if these characteristics are of value to you, the gap should be nonexistent.

Gaps and standards go hand in hand. If you aren’t settling within your relationship, then you won’t be encountering gaps. And if you find yourself with a gap in a part of your life that’s important to you, ditch the person widening it and find someone who fills it.

I’m not saying all the aforementioned gaps are going to be equally important to you. Maybe you don’t care if your partner is funny, or if they’re of your same political affiliation.

If you do care, finding someone who isn’t your complete opposite may be your saving grace. I think “opposites attract” is only somewhat true. There’s no way it’s going to work if you are completely different people, with different morals, interests and beliefs.

It should, however, be a deal breaker to come to a point in your relationship where you sacrifice your dignity. True character cannot be replaced by the facade of charisma, and if you have self-respect, the former should carry more weight.

And yes, I hear how this all sounds. All my advice ends with the same thing: Break up. I’m guilty of throwing around the phrase at any inconvenience, whether it’s a friend complaining over coffee about their situationship or a friend crying on my couch about their partner.

We’re all way too comfortable staying in relationships where compatibility is nonexistent. Forcing an unfulfilling relationship only burdens you in the long run.

These gaps are warning signs that it’s not going to work, and we need to be paying attention. The longer you continue to settle for someone who you know in your heart isn’t right for you, the further you dig your heels into a place you aren’t meant to be.

From experience, your self-worth needs to be stronger than your feelings for them. You need to be able to recognize that gaps of kindness, humor and drive outweigh the other things they’re showing you.

The gaps may even present themselves after the two of you are done, and that’s the greatest gift of all — showing you what you no longer have to deal with.

Here’s to hoping you reflect on the gaps in your life and act accordingly.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Fire departments respond to fire at Alpha Zeta fraternity house
author: Mia Debelevich | Managing Editor
description:
Editor’s Note: This story was updated Thursday evening.

Firefighters responded to a fire at the Alpha Zeta fraternity house on West Campus Thursday afternoon after smoke was reported coming from its roof, according to Centre Region Fire Director Shawn Kauffman.

Kauffman said contractors had been working on the house when the fire broke out, and no residents were in the property. There are no reported injuries.

The cause of the fire is still unknown, according to Kauffman, but is most likely related to the construction activity. It has since been put out.

He said there is heavy damage in the attic, and the rest of the building sustained heavy water and smoke damage. The property will be uninhabitable for "a period of time."

Pleasant Gap, Undine, Boalsburg and Port Matilda fire departments were on scene alongside State College’s Alpha Fire Company.

At 4:12 p.m., Penn State sent an alert urging people to avoid the area on North Burrowes Road between Curtin Street and Pollock Road.

title: Arts Fest 2026 Thursday event, entertainment lineup
author: Jackson Malenke | The Daily Collegian
description:
Arts Fest is here for day two with more activities, art and music.

Here’s what Thursday has to offer.

Art, art and more art

The 2026 Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition is back from 10 a.m. to 7 p.m. and includes a wide range of original artworks. Shoppers can browse pottery, jewelry, paintings, photography and more.

A new Live Mural Exhibition, “Dreaming American Futures,” is joining Arts Fest this year in honor of the United States’ 250th anniversary. The exhibition is taking place all five days. Designs are based on the theme of Dreaming American Futures and an excerpt from the poem “The Hill We Climb” by Amanda Gorman.

The Street Painting exhibition will take place on the east side of South Allen Street until July 11 between the hours of 10 a.m. and 7 p.m.

People of all ages can participate in the Little Art Alley where anyone can create a piece of art in a 14 x 14 inch square for a $5 donation. For a $10 donation, attendees can get a box of pastels to take home.

Craft Beverage Garden

The garden begins on Thursday, running from noon to 8 p.m. at Sidney Friedman Park. Visitors can enjoy regional craft beverages while listening to live entertainment.

Valid identification is required to purchase a reusable cup for $10 with the first fill free.

Kids Zone

The Kids Zone is located in Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza and will be open from noon to 5 p.m. on Thursday. Kids can enjoy games, craft activities and help with mural painting. This year's mural theme is “Where Hope Blooms.”

title: Potential 2026 Penn State defensive breakout candidates
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will have many new faces on the defensive side of the ball, but it’s still littered with veteran players, leaving breakout candidates limited for the 2026 season.

Regardless, there are some budding young stars and other players primed to break out. Here’s a look at some of those candidates in Matt Campbell’s first year at the helm.

Daryus Dixson

Some could argue that Daryus Dixson already had his breakout at the end of his freshman year, but he has the chance to take an even bigger step in his sophomore year.

Dixson is expected to be a full-time starter at cornerback in a very experienced room, and with Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy around him, he has the chance to take that next step and be the lockdown cornerback the Nittany Lions have been looking for.

The “breakout” Dixson could have is more so him playing full time this year and showcasing more of the consistent cornerback that was seen in the final stretch of 2025.

Zion Tracy

Tracy is a consensus starter likely at the nickel position for Penn State. However, after his first full season as a starter, Tracy’s ceiling seems to be unlimited.

After missing time at the beginning of the year due to an injury, Tracy started nine games last season, notching 32 total tackles and two pass breakups. Tracy’s mix of speed and talent could put him in position to have a dominant 2026.

“I think Zion Tracy is one of the best football players that maybe I have coached, to be quite honest with you, in terms of talent, ability,” Campbell said in March.

Campbell’s view on Tracy showcases that he still has untapped potential that he could unlock during his senior year and become a difference maker for the defense.

Max Granville

Max Granville has already been with Penn State for two years, but he hasn’t seen any substantial playing time yet due to a season-ending injury last year. Regardless, the redshirt sophomore has always been spoken of highly.

This season, Granville could step into one of the two starting defensive end spots and be a key contributor in a role that many current NFL pass rushers have succeeded in.

Granville’s attention to detail and film study on former Nittany Lions could add him to the growing list of Penn State pass rushers in the NFL like Abdul Carter, Micah Parsons and Chop Robinson, all of which he’s studied to help his game grow.

Yvan Kemajou

The pass rusher on the opposite side of Granville is likely to be Yvan Kemajou. The sophomore showed flashes of being a strong pass rusher but never garnered enough playing time to fully showcase his potential.

Kemajou began to play more and make a larger impact during the end of the year. In the final two regular season games against Nebraska and Rutgers, Kemajou notched 1.5 sacks as Terry Smith let younger players get more burn.

Now in a position to be one of the main pass rushers, Kemajou could take a major jump and grow from his freshman season to add to the lineage of edge rushers that have come through Penn State.

Armstrong Nnodim

The defensive tackle room has a lot of experience, but Armstrong Nnodim could work himself into a starting role as a younger player.

Nnodim made his way to Happy Valley after two years at Oklahoma State where he made some impact in his redshirt freshman season. The defensive tackle totaled 11 tackles and two tackles for loss in 12 games as a rotational lineman.

The redshirt sophomore has already made a jump in practice against experienced offensive linemen, and his size will be a different look than how previous Penn State defensive linemen have been built.

“You could say he's an absolute bruiser,” offensive lineman Cooper Cousins said in April. “He's hard to move. He's 315 pounds, he's got like 18% body fat. He's a freak.”

title: Top athletes at each Penn State Commonwealth campus for 2026
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sports are a key part of student life at Penn State, but University Park is just one of many campuses.

The achievements of those at the blue and white’s satellite campuses often fly under the radar, but the Penn State brand still makes noise beyond State College, stretching to every corner of the Commonwealth.

Here are the standouts from nine branch campuses, and runner-up honorable mentions.

Abington: Donato Incollingo, baseball

For a blue and white unit that finished an impressive 32-13 and went deep into the United East Tournament, Incollingo was the engine. The Huntingdon Valley, Pennsylvania, native slashed .399/.503/.842 with a team-high 17 home runs.

As for accolades, Incollingo was the 2026 United East Conference Player of the Year, an All-Region IV first team honoree by the America Baseball Coaches Association and D3baseball.com.

HM: Amirah Hackey, women’s basketball: 17.6 PPG, 5.8 RPG, 2 APG and 2.7 BPG – Lady Lions finished 20-7, United East Tournament runner-ups

Altoona: Riley Best, softball

After two seasons with Lock Haven, Best had a career year in her first season with a blue and white jersey, helping the Nittany Lions reach the Allegheny Mountain Collegiate Conference Championship Game, which they dropped to Penn State Behrend.

The Team MVP was named to the National Fastpitch Coaches Association Division III All-Region VII second-team, as well as the AMCC Player of the Year. Best led the conference in hits, home runs, RBI, slugging percentage and total bases, while finishing second in batting average.

HM: Lia Simanski, women’s volleyball: 267 kills, 22 service aces – Penn State finished 14-14, Simanski earned first team All-AMCC honors, was named Team MVP and campus Female Scholar-Athlete of the Year.

Beaver: Bailey Reig, women’s basketball

Penn State Beaver women’s basketball had an amazing season, finishing 23-6 and going deep into the postseason. The Lady Lions lost to Penn State York in the PSUAC Tournament and went on to reach the USCAA Division II National Championship.

In the blue and white’s Consolation Game victory in the bracket over Kent State-Tuscarawas, Reig scored a game-high 25 points. All season, the sophomore starred from the shooting guard position, averaging 15.2 points per game en route to earning PSUAC All-Conference honors as a member of the West First Team.

HM: Bryanna Neiberg, women’s volleyball: 240 kills, 59 SA – Penn State finished 9-17, Neiberg was named the only other USCAA All-American first team mention for Beaver outside of Reig and earned a PSUAC All-Conference second team nod.

Behrend: Dylan Barnes, baseball

The 2026 Team MVP put up all the statistics to back up the award this season, posting a .417 batting average along with a team-high seven long shots.

The Nittany Lions finished 31-12, and won the AMCC Championship Game in which Barnes had an RBI base knock. Penn State Behrend also appeared in the NCAA Division III Championship Regionals but failed to win a game. Barnes was named AMCC Player of the Year, an All-AMCC First Team selection and was named to the 2026 D3baseball.com All-Region 7 Team.

HM: Julia Jones (SB): Over 41 games played, posted team high.421 BA, also appeared on the mound, primarily as a reliever. Penn State finished 28-13 and was crowned AMCC Tournament Champions.

Berks: Talan Nguyen, men’s tennis

Penn State Behrend had a relatively successful season, going 7-6 and making it to the semifinals of the United East Championship Tournament before falling to Penn State Harrisburg.

The No. 1 singles player for the Nittany Lions all season was Nguyen, who was named to the UEC first-team as a singles player and the second-team with his doubles partner, Jazae Maina-Itegi.

HM: Maura Gorman, softball: - .299 BA, 10 RBI, .402 OBP (team best), 3.61 ERA, 54 1/3 IP, and 29 K. Penn State finished 10-21, lost to Wilson College in the UEC Tournament.

Brandywine: April Lewandowski, softball

As the lone UEC first team selection for the Brandywine softball team, she anchored a pitching staff that complemented a lineup that earned the campus's first-ever national personal awards.

Despite that batting stardom, Lewandowski was the most crucial to the blue and white’s successes. With a team-high 123 2/3 innings pitched, she was a workhorse in the circle, posting a 2.38 ERA with 116 strikeouts in the process. The highlight of Lewandowski’s season came with a no-hitter and a perfect game, both against Cedar Crest, once in the regular season and once in the postseason.

HM: Jayden Fogg, (MBB): Earned campus Male Athlete of the Year and second team All-United East honors, en route to Penn State finishing 17-10 and claiming a share of the regular season title. Averaged 11.5 PPG and 5.7 RPG.

DuBois: Natalie Bowser, women’s basketball & softball

Bowser was a two-sport athlete for DuBois, yet unlike some, she thrived at both this season. Primarily, her success came on the court, where she averaged a double-double, with 2.44 blocks per game, which earned her USCAA All-American First Team honors and PSUAC West Player of the Year.

On the diamond, Bowser posted a .354 batting average, a team high nine home runs and a tied team best 28 RBI.

HM: Isaac Tiracorda (BB): Penn State finished 32-8, with a USCAA Small College World Series appearance. The sophomore slashed .430/.551.729 with seven home runs and 38 RBI in 37 games. Named USCAA SCWS Postseason first team All-American.

Fayette: Kylie Stevenson, softball

Penn State may have only finished 8-18-1, yet despite the bumps, the Nittany Lions found their form in the postseason behind Stevenson.

The blue and white captured its first-ever PSUAC Tournament Championship, where Stevenson smashed three hits and two RBI. The Nittany Lions earned a berth in the USCAA Tournament, but couldn’t advance. Stevenson posted a .571 batting average with 27 RBI on the season.

HM: Jesse Lawson Jr., men’s basketball: Only 10 GP, 19.7 PPG, 3.7 RPG and 1.9APG, was named to PSUAC West Division First Team.

Greater Allegheny: Eric Valentin, baseball

In a successful season on the diamond, Valentin led the campaign from the batter’s box. The junior posted a team high nine home runs, 39 RBI and .420 batting average. Valentin racked in the personal accolades behind that strong year at the plate, earning USCAA First Team All-American honors and being named the PSUAC Baseball West Player of the Year.

HM: Linda Shepard, softball: Posted a .440 BA, 39 RBI, 9HR, 2.68 ERA, 86 1/3 IP and 51K. Named PSUAC Softball Player of the Year.

title: Thunderstorms expected to impact Arts Fest weekend
author: Michelle Diamond | News Editor
description:
As another day of Arts Fest 2026 concludes, sporadic thunderstorms are expected to impact the rest of the week.

Thursday is forecasted by AccuWeather to be humid with a high of 84 degrees. A shower is expected in the morning, followed by a thunderstorm in the afternoon.

Friday is forecasted by AccuWeather to be cloudy and humid with a high of 80 degrees. Come late morning, an estimated four hours of thunderstorms and rain is expected to begin.

Saturday is forecasted by AccuWeather to bring a scattering of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The high is expected to be 82 degrees.

Sunday is forecasted by AccuWeather to have a high of 82 degrees with intervals of clouds and sunshine.

Accuweather recommends attendees bring an umbrella.
